metadata.split_role: Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.

records.culinary_uses: Not a foraging target here. Pale Tylopilus species are better treated as comparison mushrooms until ID and local relevance are nailed down.

mushroom_profile.summary: Whitish Tylopilus comparison species; useful mainly for avoiding confusion with pale edible boletes.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.mushroom_card_note: Whitish Tylopilus in the bitter-bolete neighborhood; included mostly as a caution/comparison record.
records.field_identification: Pale to whitish cap, pinkish-maturing pores, hardwood association, and Tylopilus-style spore/pore colors put this near the bitter-bolete comparison lane.
records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Current confidence, food value, or field separation is not strong enough for a food recommendation. Keep searchable for comparison and field ID.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as an edible target in this guide. Use as an ID/comparison species around Tylopilus and bitter bolete groups.

mushroom_profile.summary: Brownish Tylopilus comparison species; useful for separating pink-pored/bitter allies from porcini-type boletes.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.mushroom_card_note: Brownish Tylopilus comparison species; not a basket-filler, more a reminder that pink-pored boletes get complicated.
records.field_identification: Brown to tobacco-toned cap, Tylopilus-style pinkish pore maturity, and hardwood association separate it from porcini-like boletes; taste and spore-print group matter.
records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Current confidence, food value, or field separation is not strong enough for a food recommendation. Keep searchable for comparison and field ID.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.culinary_uses: Good porcini-type edible when confidently identified and fresh; keep it separate from bitter Tylopilus and other edulis-group look-alikes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern hardwood porcini ally with dry tan cap, pale pores, non-staining flesh, and a prominent reticulate stem.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall under oaks and other hardwoods.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
pore_color: White pores becoming yellowish to olive

records.edibility_detail: Good edible reputation, but this is still an ID-first porcini-group bolete.
records.field_identification: Dry tan to grayish-brown cap, white young pores becoming yellowish/olive, non-bruising white flesh, and a prominent reticulate stem under oaks or other hardwoods.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus variipes
use_links.notes: Oak/hardwood association, non-bruising pores, and reticulate stem.

records.edibility_notes: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition.

edible_use.notes: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition.

records.duplicate_note: Legacy alias; use the visible Boletus variipes/fagicola record.
records.mushroom_card_note: Varied-stalk Bolete: eastern hardwood porcini ally with a dry tan cap and a notably reticulate stem.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bitter Tylopilus with violet/lilac tones when young; a caution/comparison species.
staining: Cap bruising rusty purple to dark purple possible
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
stem_feature: Young stem with purple to lilac shades

records.mushroom_card_note: Bitter Tylopilus with violet/lilac tones when young; useful to recognize because pretty does not mean edible.
records.field_identification: Young stem shows purple to lilac shades; cap can bruise rusty purple to dark purple, with Tylopilus pinkish pore maturity and bitter taste.
records.edibility_detail: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.
records.risk_reason: The main issue is bitterness and bitter-bolete confusion; it is included so a bitter Tylopilus does not ruin a meal.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.

records.culinary_uses: Inedible/bitter; do not collect for the table.

mushroom_profile.summary: Violet-gray Tylopilus that ages browner and tastes bitter; not a food species.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.mushroom_card_note: Violet-gray bitter bolete: striking when young, but bitter and not a food species.
records.field_identification: Young fruitbodies are violet to violet-gray, aging brownish; white pores become pinkish, flesh does not stain much, and taste is bitter.
records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Current confidence, food value, or field separation is not strong enough for a food recommendation. Keep searchable for comparison and field ID.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a prime food in this guide; useful mainly for serious bolete sorting.
records.notes: Xanthoconium purpureum: purplish-red hardwood bolete close to X. affine; chemical reaction and cap color matter.

mushroom_profile.summary: Xanthoconium purpureum: purplish-red hardwood bolete close to X. affine; chemical reaction and cap color matter.
mushroom_profile.season_note: June through September; an early-summer through fall oak/hickory bolete in the Midwest and eastern North America.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Purplish to reddish-brown cap, cream-to-yellow-brown pores, no olive pore shades, and green flash with ammonia help separate it from X. affine.

records.mushroom_card_note: Xanthoconium purpureum: purplish-red hardwood bolete close to X. affine; chemical reaction and cap color matter.
records.field_identification: Purplish to reddish-brown cap, cream-to-yellow-brown pores, no olive pore shades, and green flash with ammonia help separate it from X. affine.
records.edibility_detail: Occasional/niche at best; identification can be technical.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target here; keep as a comparison species in the small Xerocomus group.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small eastern hardwood Xerocomus with reddish-brown cap and reticulate stem; close to X. tenax.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern North American sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.mushroom_card_note: Small eastern hardwood Xerocomus with reddish-brown cap, yellow pores, and a reticulate upper stem; compare closely with X. tenax.
records.field_identification: Hardwood-associated; reddish-brown cap, yellow to olive pores, coarse reticulation near the stem apex, little to no blue staining, and blue-green ammonia reaction on the cap.
records.edibility_detail: Use as an ID/comparison record, not a beginner edible.

records.culinary_uses: Not a target edible in this guide; useful as a comparison species for small hardwood Xerocomus.

mushroom_profile.summary: Oak/hardwood Xerocomus close to X. illudens, with broad stem reticulation and blue-green ammonia reaction on the cap.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern North American sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.mushroom_card_note: Xerocomus tenax: oak/hardwood species close to X. illudens, with a tougher hold on the ground and a broadly reticulate stem.
records.field_identification: Hardwood-associated; yellowish-brown to reddish-brown cap, yellow pores, tough solid stem, broad reticulation, and blue-green ammonia reaction on the cap.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as ID/comparison, not a basket species.

records.culinary_uses: Sometimes treated as edible, but this guide keeps it cautious because the North American group is complex.

mushroom_profile.summary: Velvety yellow-brown hardwood bolete with yellow pores and possible blue bruising; likely a complex.
staining: Often bruises blue, sometimes not bruising
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
cap_surface: Finely velvety yellowish to brown cap
stem_feature: Tiny reddish granules or ribbing near apex possible

records.edibility_detail: Use only with confident ID; not a beginner target.
records.field_identification: Finely velvety yellowish to brown cap, yellow to olive-yellow pores, possible blue bruising, hardwood association, and reddish granules/ribbing near the stem apex.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Xerocomus subtomentosus aggregate
use_links.notes: Hardwood association, yellow pores, reddish granules, ribbing, and variable blue staining.

records.edibility_notes: Often listed as edible, but this is a species-group entry; use only young, firm specimens after careful ID.

edible_use.notes: Often listed as edible, but this is a species-group entry; use only young, firm specimens after careful ID.

records.mushroom_card_note: Suede Bolete group: velvety yellow-brown hardwood bolete with yellow pores and possible blue bruising; likely a species complex.
